#hiring Project Manager

Apply here: https://lnkd.in/gah5y45J

Introduction Saab UK is part of Scandinavias largest defence company, bringing together the best of Swedish and British innovation. Saab offers world-leading solutions and services in defence, aviation, space, and civil security to keep people and society safe. Our UK presence has been growing at pace, meaning we can offer a wide range of opportunities for personal fulfilment and career growth. We currently employ over 600 people across eight sites in the UK, and our specialisations include software engineering, underwater robotics, radars, AI, and armed forces training.The Role:This role is part of our Sensor Systems Business Unit.Following the planned expansion of UK-based radar manufacturing capability, and to support the delivery of the latest generation of sensors to the UK MOD and other global customers, this Project Manager role will be responsible for the management, tracking, administration and reporting of internal and external projects. This role is vital to ensure successful project delivery, monitoring of project timelines and milestones, accurate data capture and reporting, and invoicing and payment for our family of radar systems, communication systems and data links. The role will be primarily home-based with regular work at our Fareham facility. The ability and willingness to travel, including internationally, will be essential for this role.Key Responsibilities:Project management of the delivery of Saab systems to customers and users;Administering and reporting the running of projects and contracts as required;Identifying schedules, scopes, budget estimations, and implementation plans, including risk management;Coordinating internal and external resources to ensure that projects adhere to scope, schedule, and budget as well as ensuring that goals are fulfilled;Analysing project status and, when necessary and with appropriate consultation, revising the scope, schedule, or budget to ensure that project requirements can be met;Identifying opportunities for the organisation to continuously improve in order to contribute to increased efficiency in our operations;Establishing and maintaining relationships with relevant stakeholders, providing day-to-day contact on project status and changes.Essential Qualifications and Experience:Familiarity with Project Management and MIS tools

Many businesses say they’re “not hiring right now.”

But look closer.

They’re still launching projects.
Still under pressure to reduce costs.
Still expected to improve delivery.
Still navigating AI, digital change, compliance, and operational strain.

What’s often paused is permanent headcount, not business demand.

That’s why more leaders are quietly turning to:
• Fixed-term specialists
• Independent consultants
• Fractional leadership
• Project-based contractors
• Contract-to-perm models

Not because it’s trendy.

Because it solves a real business problem.

The smartest hiring strategy in 2026 may not be hiring more employees.

It may be accessing the right capability at the right time.

If delivery cannot wait, your talent model shouldn’t slow you down.

#FlexibleHiring #Contractors #FutureOfWork #TalentStrategy #Leadership #HiringTrends #Recruitment #BusinessGrowth #OutsideCapital

We are experiencing a significant pivot towards contract and project based hiring, driven by a need for agility, AI implementation, and cost control, despite a generally subdued wider economic backdrop.

Talking to our clients across various roles in HR Tech and Senior Management    they have told us that the increased demand for contractors has arose to avoid long term hiring commitments amid economic uncertainty.

The tech sector has resilience during these times along with engineering, electronics, and advanced manufacturing, demand remains high compared to other sectors, with above normal demand for contract specialists.

The surge in demand has been created by the urgency to embed AI, upgrade legacy systems, and strengthen cyber resilience within organisations in order to remain competitive and protect their users.

Niche and specialized skills are in the highest demand, including AI/ML engineering, data engineering, cloud security (AWS, Azure), Cyber Security (CISSP, CISM), DevOps, and Data Governance.

 Short term and fixed price digital projects are dominating the market, particularly for cloud migrations and data platform builds. 

If your organisation is looking for a contractor we can help you send us an email or call to discuss what you need.

🗼 Tokyo’s cloud market is entering a very different phase.

For years, most conversations were around migration. Lift and shift, cost optimisation, basic modernisation.

Now it has changed.

The discussions I am having with clients today are more like:

💎 How do we scale infrastructure for AI driven workloads?
💎 How do we handle performance, latency, and resilience at scale?
💎 How do we find people who can actually build this?

Because the investment going into Japan right now is huge.

🪩 Amazon Web Services has committed over $15B into Japan’s cloud infrastructure.
🪩 Microsoft is investing another $10B while targeting 1M people trained in AI and cloud skills.

On the domestic side:

📍 SoftBank Group is building large scale AI and compute infrastructure.
📍 Nippon Telegraph and Telephone is pushing next generation data networks and energy efficient data centres.
📍 Fujitsu continues to expand hybrid and high performance computing capabilities.

At the same time, Japan’s data centre market is projected to reach around $38B by 2031.

So demand is not the issue.

Execution is.

There is still heavy reliance on legacy systems.
Power and land constraints are real, especially around Tokyo.
And the talent pool for cloud infrastructure at scale is tight.

Japan is moving, but it is moving carefully.

From a hiring perspective, the pressure points are clear:

👉 Cloud architects
👉 Platform and infrastructure engineers
👉 DevOps and SRE
👉 Cloud security and compliance

This is not early adoption anymore.
This is scale.

And scale creates urgency.

At Morgan McKinley, we support companies across Japan to scale their cloud teams, particularly through high impact contract talent when speed matters.

We also support urgent hiring when teams need to scale quickly or when there is a business critical gap that cannot wait.

Please feel free to message me directly or email muhammadimdadkhan@morganmckinley.com

Curious how others are seeing this shift on the ground.

#CloudComputing #TokyoTech #JapanTech #CloudJobs #TechHiring #TechTalent #HiringInJapan #CloudCareers #ScalableSystems #EnterpriseIT #FutureOfWork #ITRecruitment #MorganMcKinley

As the FY26 annual reports roll out across the Indian B2B landscape this month, the market is quietly burying a five-year-old thesis: The "Discovery Premium."

For years, industrial aggregators raised massive rounds by simply digitizing the Rolodex. If you could build a platform that connected enterprise buyers with fragmented tier-3 suppliers, you were rewarded with a software-like valuation.

However, today, simply acting as a digital middleman is a fast track to severe valuation compression.

But the most critical takeaway from this earnings season isn't the death of the pure marketplace model, but it is the pivot.

The most formidable players in the space are abandoning the "asset-light" illusion and aggressively moving into digital contract manufacturing. They have realized that in major industrial hubs like the NCR, finding a supplier isn't the actual problem. The friction is guaranteeing the production quality, standardizing the defect rate, and ensuring strict delivery timelines.

The new, defensible moat is no longer just financing the transaction. It is taking absolute operational control of the supply chain physics.

What this forces is a complete teardown of how these organizations are built.

When we were brought in to consult with a few of the top brands in the aggregator space at Straffing AI last year, we warned their leadership boards that this exact margin collapse was imminent. We advised them that surviving this wasn't just a matter of changing the sales pitch; it required a ruthless rewiring of their entire talent architecture, from acquisition and mobility to development and retention.

The firms that listened made the hard choices early. They stopped hiring armies of network coordinators to boost vanity platform listings. Instead, they aggressively pivoted their acquisition strategy to poach hardcore supply chain operators, quality engineers, and execution-focused leaders who can control a factory floor they don't even own.

The result? While the rest of the market is currently bleeding capital trying to hire for a marketplace that mathematically no longer exists, the teams that executed this talent pivot built the exact operational foundation required to stabilize their margins. They are hitting their efficiency targets quarters ahead of schedule because they finally have the right operators driving the machine.

So, if your B2B model just introduces a buyer to a seller, your margins are trending toward zero. The next decade of industrial dominance belongs entirely to the operators who take full responsibility for the outcome.

🚨 Why Uncertainty is the Biggest Challenge for UAE Businesses in 2026 — And How Smart Hiring Wins
Uncertainty is no longer a phase — it’s the operating environment.

Across the UAE, businesses aren’t struggling because of a lack of opportunities…
They’re struggling because they can’t predict what’s next.
In today’s market, hiring is not about filling roles —
it’s about making the right decision at the right time.

🔍 How uncertainty is impacting HR & recruitment:
🔹 Hiring Delays
Decision-making cycles are longer. Roles stay open not due to lack of talent — but lack of clarity.
🔹 Cautious Workforce Planning
Every hire is evaluated based on ROI, not urgency. Bulk hiring is being replaced with precision hiring.
🔹 Talent Mismatch
Companies are looking for multi-skilled, AI-aware, adaptable professionals — raising the hiring bar significantly.
🔹 Retention Pressure
Employees seek stability, growth, and strong leadership — not just salary.
🔹 Budget Constraints
HR teams are expected to deliver better results with tighter budgets — faster, smarter, and more efficient hiring.

💡 What smart companies are doing differently:
✔ Hiring critical roles only — not volume hiring
✔ Investing in high-impact talent over average profiles
✔ Adopting flexible workforce strategies (contract/project-based)
✔ Partnering with experts to reduce hiring risk & time

👉 The reality:
This is not a bad market — it’s a selective market.
Companies that adapt now…
will dominate when stability returns.

💡 This is exactly where we step in.
At VGETTER HR, we understand that in an uncertain market,
you don’t need more candidates — you need the right ones.
✔ High-impact, role-specific hiring
✔ Reduced hiring risk through deep screening & market insight
✔ Faster closures even in slow decision cycles
✔ Talent aligned with business goals — not just job descriptions

👉 Our approach is simple:
We don’t just fill positions.
We help you make confident, strategic hiring decisions — even in uncertainty.

📌 Because in 2026:
The companies that win are not the ones hiring more…
They are the ones hiring smarter.

🔁 If you’re hiring in the UAE, ask yourself:
Are you reacting to uncertainty… or strategically using it?

Sound familiar?

That’s exactly why we built AskHRM.com 💼
👉 Your AI-powered HR & Labour Law assistant

 👉 Stay compliant with South African labour law
 👉 Avoid costly mistakes and legal headaches

💬 Whether you’re dealing with:
 • Disciplinary hearings
 • Employee misconduct
 • Contracts & policies
 • Leave, wages & disputes
 • Or just need quick HR guidance…

AskHRM has the answers — instantly.

🔥 Get 3 FREE expert consultations every day
No delays. No expensive consultants.

Just clear, practical HR support when you need it.
🌐 Try it now: askhrm.com

The biggest change AI has brought to the businesses is building lean teams.

For the legal teams as well, it is no different! It finally showed founders what they were overpaying for.

Drafting standard clauses. Summarizing agreements. Formatting NDAs. AI does it now. Faster. Cheaper.

And honestly? That's a good thing. Because it clears the noise and surfaces the real question every scaling founder should be asking:

When something goes wrong in a contract, who's got your back?
Not a tool. Not a template. A person who knows your business, your deals, and your risk appetite.

Here's what I see with startups that scale fast:
They're signing more contracts than ever. Cross-border deals. Investor agreements. Vendor lock-ins. And most of them? Reviewed quickly, not reviewed well.

That's where exposure quietly builds up.
The smartest founders in 2026 aren't hiring full-time GCs they can't afford yet. 

And they're not winging it with AI and templates either.

They're bringing in fractional in-house legal, and here's why it works:
✔️ You get GC-level thinking, not associate-level execution 
✔️ Your legal partner learns your business, not just your documents 
✔️ You pay for outcomes, not hours 
✔️ Risk is managed continuously, not reactively
AI will keep getting better at the what of legal work. 

The so what still needs a human in the room.
The future of legal isn't more lawyers. It's better-placed legal judgment, exactly when and where it matters.

If you're scaling and struggling with figuring out the legal stuff, let's talk. DMs are open.

They bought the software. They trusted the vendor. They never asked what it was actually doing.
 
Now someone is asking them.And the question is always the same.
How was this decision made?
Right now, in most organizations, the honest answer is:
The system decided. In seconds. And we trusted it.
That answer is not a defense. It's an admission.
Because here's what nobody told you when you bought the software — speed without documentation isn't efficiency. It's liability you haven't been invoiced for yet.
The ATS didn't file anything. The algorithm didn't sign anything. The AI didn't agree to produce records in discovery.
You did.
And when the investigation opens, they're not asking what tool you used. They're asking for the decision trail. The audit log. The bias assessment. The candidate notification records. The evidence that a human was actually in the loop.
Most organizations don't have it.
Not because they're malicious. Because they purchased a system, plugged it into their hiring process, and assumed they fell outside the definition of an Automated Employment Decision Tool.
They never investigated whether that assumption was correct. They never asked what the tool was actually doing to candidates. They never checked whether their use case triggered a compliance obligation.
They just trusted the vendor. And the vendor had a demo, a contract, and a very confident account manager.
And for multi-jurisdiction companies it compounds. A completed LL144 audit doesn't touch your EU AI Act obligations. A EU conformity assessment doesn't satisfy LL144. Having one doesn't cancel the other.
They thought compliance was a checkbox.
It was never a checkbox.
The machine was fast. The documentation wasn't there.
That gap — between the 11 seconds it took to reject a candidate and the evidence trail that never existed — is where the liability lives.
It doesn't matter which framework finds it first.
Have you audited what your hiring system is actually deciding?

New financial year. New challenges.

For many businesses, uncertainty has changed the equation.

AI is moving fast.
Budgets are tighter.
Permanent hiring decisions feel heavier.
And the long-term cost of today’s tooling choices is still far from settled.

That hesitation is understandable.

When conditions change, the old assumptions around hiring, investment, pace and risk change with them.

And while AI is influencing a lot of decision-making, not every AI-led move is automatically a good one. In many cases, organisations are still experimenting, still learning, and still trying to separate genuine value from market noise.

There is also a more practical question that does not get enough attention:
What happens when today’s attractive platform, tooling or AI pricing shifts tomorrow?

AI licensing costs will rise over time.

So before businesses go too far down the AI path, it is worth asking whether the long-term commercial model has really been thought through, or whether the current phase is still more experimentation than strategy.

At the same time, transformation is rarely just about buying new technology.
Operational complexity matters.

New platforms, cloud shifts, AI tooling, data flows, governance models, supplier dependencies, technical debt, delivery friction and commercial accountability all have to work together in the real world. That is often where progress slows, costs rise and confidence drops.

That is one reason why contract and fractional leadership can make a lot of sense in this market.

It gives businesses access to experience without forcing a long-term commitment too early.

It also creates space for targeted, time-boxed support around specific outcomes, whether that is shaping a new initiative, steadying delivery, navigating operational transformation, or helping bridge the gap between cloud-led innovation and the operational, commercial and delivery realities of telecoms.

For organisations working across cloud, telecoms and adjacent digital platforms, that can be the difference between momentum and drift.

I am open to conversations where experienced support is needed across solution architecture, technical delivery, growth leadership and technical sales.

#SolutionArchitecture #TechnicalDelivery #FractionalLeadership #Telecoms #CloudTransformation

Philips has had R&D engineers in Bangalore for years. Heineken opened a Hyderabad hub. Rabobank, TomTom, AkzoNobel, FrieslandCampina, Randstad all run their own capability centres in India.

For Dutch companies this is not new. What is changing is why it matters.
The early argument was cost. What these firms ended up with was capability and ownership. That turns out to matter more now that AI is on the table.

AI works best inside processes you actually control. Not because vendors cannot use AI, but because the real gains come from redesigning the process itself, and that is hard to do when the work sits under a fixed-scope contract with someone else.

The Dutch talent numbers are not getting easier either. Around 45,000 STEM graduates a year against 2.5 million in India. Hiring cycles of three to five months in Amsterdam against four to ten weeks in Hyderabad.

None of this makes a GCC the right answer for every company. It does make it a real question worth thinking through.

At Airopa Global the team has have put together a whitepaper on how Dutch companies can approach this. Worth a read if it is on your desk.

One in Five Unions Has AI Contract Protections. Here's What the Other Four Are Demanding in 2026.

Only 20% of unions worldwide have secured AI-specific provisions in collective bargaining agreements. But 42% are actively negotiating them right now.

That gap is the defining labor relations story of 2026 — and it's landing on HR's desk.

Here's what unions are demanding at the table:

→ Advance notice before AI tools are deployed that affect scheduling, performance reviews, or task allocation
→ Transparency about how algorithmic decisions are made — what data goes in, what triggers a flag
→ Right to a clear, human-readable explanation when AI directly affects a worker's conditions

These aren't theoretical. Fenway Park concession workers ratified an AI automation clause in December 2025. Spain's banking sector has AI provisions in sector-level agreements. The Colorado AI Act (effective June 2026) adds statutory pressure that unions can now use as a bargaining floor.

For HR leaders, three actions matter most before your next cycle:

1. Map every AI tool touching unionized roles — scheduling, evals, task allocation, hiring
2. Build internal disclosure protocols now, before you reach the table
3. Develop human-readable explanation templates for your highest-impact AI systems

The 20% of unions that already have AI protections are setting the terms for the next 20% — and the 20% after that.

If your AI screening tool systematically penalises CVs with employment gaps, you may be indirectly discriminating against women returning from maternity leave, people with long-term disabilities, and specific ethnic demographics. That's the warning the ICO effectively made in March 2026.

Here's what quietly changed in the legal backdrop in 2026, and why HR teams need to pay attention:

• The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 came fully into force on 5 February 2026. It replaced Article 22's blanket prohibition on automated decision-making with a legitimate-interests framework.

• The ICO's updated AI and Biometrics Strategy explicitly states they'll scrutinise the use of automated decision-making in recruitment in 2026, looking specifically at transparency, discrimination and redress.

• The ICO consultation on draft ADM guidance runs until 29 May 2026, with updated recruitment and selection guidance landing later this year.

• The Equality Act 2010 remains the sharpest risk. AI doesn't get a pass — if a tool produces a discriminatory outcome, the employer is liable.

The trap most SMBs walk into isn't malicious. It's this: you buy an AI screening tool, it works "well enough", and six months later you've made 80 hiring decisions with no documented human review, no Data Protection Impact Assessment, and no way to explain to a candidate why they were screened out.

A seven-point audit to run before the end of this quarter:

1. Map every AI touchpoint in your hiring funnel — including CV screening, video interview analysis, and chatbots
2. Run a DPIA before you deploy, not after an ICO letter arrives
3. Ask your vendor whose CVs the model was trained on
4. Define what "meaningful human review" actually looks like in your process
5. Write candidate-facing transparency notices
6. Test for adverse impact across protected characteristics
7. Set a retraining and monitoring cadence — at least quarterly

AI in recruitment is lawful in the UK. Careless AI in recruitment isn't. The difference is mostly documentation — and whether anyone has actually read the vendor contract.

Full 7-point audit checklist: https://lnkd.in/e6qGazYU

Most companies don’t have an “AI strategy.”

But most companies already use AI every day.
Your email client suggests replies. Your CRM scores leads automatically. Your recruitment tool filters CVs before a human ever sees them. Your marketing platform optimizes ad spend in real time. Your support chatbot handles the first round of customer questions.

None of this requires a data science team. These are off-the-shelf tools that millions of SMEs across Europe rely on daily.

The question is no longer “are we using AI?” - it’s “do our teams understand what they’re using?”
When an employee pastes confidential client data into ChatGPT to “quickly summarize” a contract, that’s an AI literacy gap. When a hiring manager trusts an AI-filtered shortlist without understanding how the filter works, that’s a governance gap.

AI adoption happened. AI understanding hasn’t caught up yet.

🚨 Layoffs in tech are making headlines again.

In Malaysia alone, over 24,000 workers were retrenched in Q1 2026 — a 47% increase year-on-year.
Selangor and Kuala Lumpur remain the most affected regions.
In March itself, 5,900 job losses were recorded, with Selangor contributing close to 30% of cases.

📊 Source: Malaysia labour market insights reported via DOSM (Department of Statistics Malaysia) and covered by Says.com

But here’s what’s interesting from the hiring side… 👇
Companies are still struggling to find the right IT talent.
Sounds contradictory—but this is exactly what we’re seeing across multiple clients in banking, fintech, and telco sectors.
💡 The reality is — hiring demand hasn’t dropped. It has shifted.
There’s less need for generalist roles, but a clear spike in demand for niche skill sets like:
• 🤖 AI / Data
• ☁️ Cloud & DevOps
• 🔐 Cybersecurity
At the same time, companies are becoming more cautious:
• Leaner team structures
• More contract-based hiring
• Strong preference for candidates who can deliver from Day 1

⚠️ This is creating a real gap in the market:
More candidates available… but fewer who meet current hiring expectations.

📌 Personal insight:
We’re seeing 2–3x longer hiring cycles for niche tech roles, even when there are more candidates in the market.

From a recruitment standpoint, this is where the challenge (and opportunity) lies — aligning the right talent to evolving business needs.
The market isn’t slowing down.
It’s becoming more selective.

Curious to hear — are you seeing the same shift in your hiring strategy this year?

The performance marketing agency that took our money and delivered zero results.
Let me save you the same expensive mistake.
Here's what happened:
We hired an agency. Big promises:
"We'll 5x your ROAS"
"We've worked with top D2C jewellery brands" ( Jewellery is the keyword here...) 
"Just give us 3 months"

Cost: ₹XL 

Results: Literally nothing

The red flags I ignored:
1. They didn't ask about our customers
First call: "What's your budget?"
Not: "Who are you selling to?"
If an agency doesn't want to understand your customer, they're just going to burn your money on bad targeting. ( they actually did ) 

2. Their strategy was just a CHAT-GPT basic version of "how to run ads" 
And when I questioned them, after vetting it from the tool itself 
It was labelled as "Micro-managing the strategy" 
"Not trusting your agency partner"

There's no harm in using AI - but atleast put it to use intelligently! The moment you spot this, just Run.

3. They wanted a long-term contract upfront
3-month minimum. No trial. No proof.
Good agencies prove value first, then ask for commitment.

4. Their "case studies" were complete false, atleast what it looked like! 
"We grew a jewelry brand 300%!"
They showed me the brand 
Shopify screenshots, but when I spoke to the founder herself on IG, everything came out to be false! 

Lesson = Shopify dashboards can be misleading 

5. They didn't want to integrate with our team
"Just give us your ad account and we'll handle it."
Translation: "We don't want you to see what we're actually doing."

What I learned (the expensive way):
1. Small, scrappy > big agency
That ₹XL? I could've hired a freelancer and gotten better results.
Agencies have overhead. You're paying for their office, not just your campaigns.

2. Start with a test project
Never commit long-term until you've seen results.
Pay for one month. One campaign. One deliverable.
Prove it works, THEN scale.

3. Ask for their actual process
Good agencies will walk you through exactly how they work.
Bad agencies will use jargon and promise magic.

4. Check if they've worked in your category
Fashion/jewelry ≠ SaaS ≠ B2B.
If they've never done your category, you're paying them to learn on your dime.

What I'd do differently:
→ Hire a freelance expert for 1 month (test)
 → Ask for weekly reports (not monthly black boxes)
 → Start small budget, scale what works
 → Never sign long contracts upfront
 → Trust my gut (if it feels off, it probably is)

If you're considering hiring an agency, Ask these questions BEFORE signing:
"Can you show me actual campaigns you've run in my category?"
"What's your process for the first 30 days?"
"Can we do a 1-month test before committing long-term?"
"Who will actually be working on my account?" (not the person selling)
"What metrics will you track and how often will I see them?"

If they can't answer clearly? Don't hire them.

Anyone else been burned by a bad agency?
Drop your story below. Let's save each other some money.

Your agents understand context, not just commands.

That’s the difference most people feel, but can’t quite explain.

With most AI tools, you have to be very specific.

You type a command.
You get a response.

Then you adjust it.
Try again.
Rewrite it.

Because the tool doesn’t fully understand what you’re actually trying to do.

It’s reacting.

Not working with you.

That’s where ProPrompt is different.

Your AI agents don’t just respond to inputs.

They understand the context around the work.

What you’re looking at.
What you’re trying to achieve.
What step you’re on.

So instead of repeating yourself or starting over, you can just show them what you’re working on.

And they help you move forward.

What that looks like in practice:

• Less explaining every step
• Fewer repeated prompts
• More relevant outputs
• Faster progress through tasks

It feels more natural.

More aligned.

More like actual support.

From hiring to sales to operations, the work becomes easier because the agent understands what’s happening around it, not just what you type.

You can try it for yourself with a 14-day free trial at https://proprompt.store

No credit card. No lock-in contracts. Just see how it works in your workflow.

Because real work doesn’t happen in isolated commands.

It happens in context.

With Q2 now well and truly underway I’ve been reflecting on what’s been a busy and strong Q1 for our team. 
 
Across the team, we’ve delivered on a number of exclusive projects, partnered with new clients and continued to build on existing relationships. We’ve also been out and about, attending and hosting multiple events with lots more planned for Q2 and beyond.
 
 Q1 has shown the NI tech market isn’t slowing but it is evolving. 
 A few key trends stood out: 
 
 • AI is reshaping hiring priorities
 AI is now a real factor in hiring decisions. We are seeing teams evolve rather than shrink, engineers are expected to work more efficiently, with greater emphasis on problem-solving, architecture, and leveraging AI tools effectively. Teams are being built around AI-enabled delivery, not just traditional development.

 • Mid–Senior talent still in highest demand
 Experienced hires remain the priority, particularly those who can add immediate impact in smaller teams

 • Higher expectations, not fewer roles
 AI and automation aren’t reducing demand, but they are raising the bar.
 Employers want people who can deliver impact quickly, adapt, and work across multiple domains. Purely siloed roles are becoming less common.

 • Contract hiring 
 With cost pressures and evolving legislation in the background, businesses are leaning into flexibility. The contract market is stable with signs of recovery and flexible resourcing is becoming a core strategy.

 • Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning and Cloud Computing continued to be the most in-demand skills. Systems Implementation and Virtualisation both emerged as in-demand areas, reflecting the continued push for digital transformation, scalability, and cost efficiency.
 
 AI isn’t reducing the need for talent but it is raising the bar. The market feels more intentional, and companies are hiring with intent with higher expectations on impact and adaptability.
 
 If you’re hiring or exploring a new job opportunity and would like to have a chat about the market please don’t hesitate to get in touch.

Most agentic AI companies don’t fail on product, they stall on getting the right access. Particularly in sovereign and regulated markets, where the largest contracts sit.

From what I’m seeing in live engagements, companies underestimate three things:

• Access is structured, not open
• You don’t “sell into” these markets in the usual way. Entry is gated by trust, relationships, and often non-commercial pathways.
• Commercial strategy alone isn’t enough
Regulation, national priorities and institutional alignment shape whether you’re even considered.
• Early signals matter more than late optimisation

If you get the first steps wrong, no amount of pipeline or hiring fixes it later.
The companies that move effectively do something different as they treat market entry as a system:

• Commercial
• Regulatory
• Partner-led
• And where needed, diplomatically supported

Get that right and access becomes repeatable but its easybto get it wrong, and you stay on the outside looking in.

This is the architecture I’ve been focused on over the past few years across GCC, Europe and the UK. Happy to discuss and interested in your opinions.

leadership brain storming chapter -119

Optimizing hotel labor costs requires a mixed-staffing model, utilizing permanent staff for core operations, alongside seasonal and casual employees to match staffing levels with demand, reducing payroll during slow periods. This strategic mix keeps fixed labor costs low while providing the agility to meet occupancy peaks without overstaffing. 

Key Staffing Types for Cost Management:

# Permanent Employees (Full-Time): Core staff ensuring service quality, consistency, and low turnover costs.

# Seasonal Contract Staffing: Hired for specific peak periods, reducing long-term financial commitments and overheads associated with permanent hiring.

# Casual/Temporary Employees: Ideal for sudden shifts in demand or short-term needs, providing maximum flexibility and reducing overtime costs.

# Cross-trained Staff: Employees trained for multiple roles allow for lean staffing, improving productivity during fluctuations. 

Cost Optimization Strategies:

# Demand-Based Scheduling: Use historical data and AI-powered forecasting to schedule staff, as outlined in.

# Dynamic Staffing: As shown by, adjusting the mix of permanent and casual workers based on daily room occupancy minimizes overstaffing costs.

# Cross-Training: Training existing employees for multiple roles increases flexibility without raising payroll.

# Developing a Talent Pool: Rehire experienced seasonal workers to reduce training costs and maintain high quality.

# Leveraging Technology: Implement software for workforce management and scheduling to optimize efficiency.

After spending few weeks automating most of Herizon workflows across multiple products, here’s the simplest framework I can give:

Clear inputs + clear outputs + low mistake cost → automate completely. Data processing, content drafts, monitoring, matching.

Repetitive but needs judgment → automate 80%, route the 20% to a human. Support, hiring pipeline, content publishing.

High stakes + context dependent → AI prepares, human decides. Contracts, crises, anything with no undo button.

Most companies are doing one of two things wrong: automating nothing or trying to automate everything. Technically you could automate almost everything hands-off with a openclaw in a box but entire skill is knowing where to put the handoff.

Day 3 of the People and Culture Accelerator brought 15 startups to the final leg of derisking their people and culture systems: Preparing to deploy countermeasures for the risks they have assessed for their startups.

The day began with a workshop led by Bhakti Jhaveri Dhanak, PHRi, SPHRi and Supriya Sharma, focusing on hiring - a key theme emerging as a risk area for all startups - as a deliberate strategy. Founders worked on building talent pipelines and making hiring decisions aligned with where their companies are headed.

This was followed by a fireside with Shaival Desai of Petpooja, focusing on leadership in practice. The conversation looked at how founders navigate decisions and trade-offs in real time, and included raw, real life snippets of how non-tech native founders navigated productivity and employee upskilling in the age of AI-automation.

The next session looked at the legal side of building organisations - another risk area founders were aware of, but they just did not know how they were exposed, and how to navigate safeguarding against legal risks in the future. Sanea Vakaliya from the IIMA Ventures unpacked the frameworks that shape how companies operate and scale - from contracts, agreements and discussions from all stakeholders, including co-founders, employees, investors, and others.

The latter half of the day brought operators (incidentally, also alumni of previous People and Culture accelerators!) into the room. A panel featuring Gaurav Goel of Toprankers, Parth Sharma of SatSure, and Prateek Sharma of Heelium talked to the founders of this cohort about how their own people and culture deployment plans fared when, and what they did to navigate unexpected hurdles. Having been on the other side of the table and part of earlier editions, they shared what holds and what breaks when theory is executed - a conversation that was more interesting because of their diverse contexts.

This extended into a working session that flipped the room, where founders applied, questioned, and stress-tested what they had built over the three days.

We closed the accelerator with a debrief and roadmap session bringing together reflections and outlining what comes next. This marked the end of the three-day bootcamp. What follows are dedicated learning sessions with these founders, tracking their progress. With the 4th edition, we continue to bring people and culture to the front.

TCS just cut fresher hiring by 43% — and the real story is bigger than the number.

India’s largest IT company has extended only 25,000 job offers to fresh graduates for FY27 — down sharply from 44,000 in FY26, which was itself one of the highest fresher intakes in the Indian private sector that year.

CEO K Krithivasan confirmed the numbers and was direct: more hiring will happen only when demand clarity improves.

Here’s what’s actually driving this shift:

→ TCS’s total headcount fell by ~24,000 in FY26, even as it hired 44,000 freshers — meaning attrition and layoffs were running parallel to campus hiring
→ 12,000 senior-level roles were cut in FY26, driven by a structural shift in how projects are executed — not AI, as Krithivasan explicitly clarified
→ Freshers need up to 9 months of training before becoming billable; laterals contribute immediately — making fresher hiring a long-term bet that depends on future demand visibility
→ TCS now operates on a demand-driven hiring model, not a talent-pooling one

The broader context: a growing share of entry-level IT tasks — basic coding, testing, debugging — is increasingly being handled by AI tools. Large IT firms are recalibrating what “entry-level” even means in this environment.

For India’s engineering graduates, this is a signal worth reading carefully.

TCS still booked $40 billion in total contract value in FY26. The business is stable. But the old model of mass campus hiring as a default strategy is clearly changing.

What does this mean for the next generation of tech talent in India?

The GenZ's wants to work remotely

The Millennial wants to work onsite

Hiring Managers want to get “the best person for the job.”

And somewhere in the middle… the workplace is quietly having an identity crisis.

Because let’s be honest—this is no longer just about where we work.

It’s about how we define work itself.

A few years ago, the “ideal job” looked predictable:
9–5, physical office, clear hierarchy, slow growth ladder.

Today? That model feels almost… outdated.


Now we have:

👉Remote-first roles that prioritize output over presence

👉Hybrid setups trying (and often struggling) to balance flexibility and control

👉Contract and freelance roles replacing “job security” with “income diversity”

👉Creator-led careers where people build personal brands instead of climbing corporate ladders

👉AI-augmented jobs where your biggest competition isn’t another human—it’s efficiency

So when Gen Z says they want remote work, it’s not just about comfort.

It’s about autonomy. Control. A rejection of rigid systems.

When Millennials push for onsite or hybrid, it’s not outdated thinking.

It’s structure. Collaboration. Stability in a world that’s constantly shifting.

And when hiring managers say they want “the best person for the job,”
what they often mean is:

👉 Someone who can deliver results
👉 Adapt quickly
👉 Communicate clearly
👉 And fit into a system that is still evolving

Here’s the uncomfortable truth most people won’t say out loud:

The job market is no longer designed to make everyone comfortable.

It’s designed to reward:

👉Adaptability over loyalty
👉Skills over tenure
👉Value creation over time spent

So the real question is not:
“Remote or onsite?”
It’s:
👉 Can you thrive in uncertainty?
👉 Can you prove your value regardless of location?
👉 Can you evolve faster than the job market itself?

Because the future of work isn’t one model replacing another.

It’s a messy, unpredictable mix of all of them

And the people who win won’t be the ones arguing about where to work…

They’ll be the ones who understand how to stay relevant, anywhere.

Curious—where do you stand right now? Remote, onsite, or somewhere in between?

The core message is a single, punchy truth that every QA/SDET on LinkedIn feels right now: QA is no longer just testing. AI hasn't replaced quality engineers — it has fundamentally elevated what the job demands. The poster lands that message fast, then backs it with data and trends that give readers something concrete to react to.

The 3 headline stats were chosen deliberately. The 70%+ developer stat (developers routinely rewrite AI-generated code before it's production-ready) is counterintuitive and attention-grabbing — it positions QA as the accountability layer that AI cannot replace. "Self-Healing" and "Agentic" anchor the two biggest tooling shifts happening right now, giving mid-career QA engineers a vocabulary upgrade they can use in their own interviews and LinkedIn conversations.

The 6 trends are grounded in what industry sources — Parasoft, Tricentis, Xray, ACCELQ — are reporting for 2026. They are not generic. Each one maps directly to a skill gap or a hiring signal in the SDET/automation market:
Agentic AI Testing — AI-driven test agents (Tricentis, Testim, etc.) are actively replacing manual trigger-based pipelines. Companies hiring SDET now ask about this in first-round interviews.

Self-Healing Frameworks — Playwright and Cypress are adding AI locator healing natively. This is no longer a premium feature — it's becoming table stakes in 2026 job descriptions.

AI-Code Validation — More than 50% of AI-generated code samples contain logical or security flaws. QA engineers are being asked to build "AI checklists" as a formal process. This is a brand new niche with almost no competition yet.
Shift-Left + Shift-Right — Testing has escaped the QA silo and now runs at every pipeline stage: static analysis at commit, API contract tests at build, real-user observability in production. SDETs who understand this full spectrum command 30–40% higher salaries.

Quality Observability — Production data is feeding back into test coverage decisions. This is the bridge between QA and SRE — a convergence that creates a premium new role profile.

AI Bias & Ethics Testing — As AI systems enter healthcare, finance, and education (your own education platform work is relevant here), QA must now validate that AI doesn't exhibit bias or unsafe behavior. Barely any QA professionals are skilled here yet — massive first-mover advantage.

The shekel hit its strongest in 30 years / USD last week. Will it kill tech exporter margins and threaten jobs in Israel? 

From the Weekend Edition - brought to you by TV10 Israel

Israel continues to attract formidable cross-border capital flow, evidenced this week by Palo Alto Networks’ strategic acquisition of Israeli AI-defender Koi, and EagleNXT’s $10 million equity injection into defense-drone manufacturer ThirdEye Systems. Yet, beneath these headline-grabbing transactions, the day-to-day operational reality for Israeli exporters is deteriorating due to a severe currency mismatch.

Global tech contracts and M&A valuations are universally denominated in US dollars. To understand the sheer scale of this currency drag, look at the recent M&A market. When Google acquired Wiz for $32 billion on March 18, 2025, that capital injection was worth roughly ₪115 billion to the local ecosystem. Today, at a sub-3.00 exchange rate, that exact same $32 billion valuation yields just ₪95 billion. That is a staggering ₪20 billion, a 17.39% contraction, in local purchasing power wiped out by FX mechanics alone.

While repatriated top-line revenue shrinks, operational expenditures remain firmly anchored in the expensive shekel. For US-based multinationals operating massive R&D hubs in Israel, the math is becoming untenable. 

These tech giants fund their global operations in dollars but pay their local workforce in shekels. With the exchange rate dropping below 3.00, the dollar-cost of maintaining those Israeli payrolls just spiked by tens of percentage points. Given that Tel Aviv software engineers are already among the most expensive in the world, this severe currency shock threatens to erase the cost-benefit of hiring locally.

If the USD/ILS pair remains depressed, multinational tech firms and local unicorns may accelerate the offshoring of R&D headcount to cheaper jurisdictions to protect their margins. 

If the threat of multinational hiring freezes or capital flight becomes severe enough, it could be the ultimate catalyst that forces the Bank of Israel’s hand, compelling the central bank to cut interest rates to devalue the shekel and save the tech sector, even at the risk of letting domestic inflation run hot.

Contract vs Perm in Project Services: What’s actually shifting in Melbourne right now? 

We’re seeing a clear reset in how businesses are hiring across transformation and change.

It’s no longer contract vs perm, it’s about when and why.
Right now:
Contract is being used surgically → to deliver critical phases, unblock programs, and bring in niche capability fast 
Perm is being used strategically → to build long-term transformation capability and embed change into the business 

And here’s the shift most aren’t talking about 👇
A lot of organisations are holding perm hiring early, leaning on contract to get moving… then converting once direction is clearer.
Why?
Because transformation itself is changing.
We’re moving from “projects” → to continuous change, heavily influenced by AI, automation, and evolving operating models.
That means:
Shorter, sharper contracts (6–12 months is the norm) 
Greater demand for specialists who can hit the ground running 
And a stronger focus on people who can lead change, not just deliver tasks 

From where I sit, the businesses getting ahead aren’t choosing one model — they’re blending both, deliberately.
Because in this market, flexibility wins.
But clarity on outcomes wins more.
Curious, what are you seeing on the ground?

Give me a call if you want to know what's happening in the Melbourne job market, or simply just send me a DM if you want to connect.

A job interviewer just asked a candidate to use AI mid-interview.

Not to test their coding.
Not to test their AI knowledge.

To see if they could THINK with it.

This happened at McKinsey — the consulting firm that shapes how the world's biggest companies operate.

And it's just the beginning.

Here's the full picture:

→ McKinsey has 25,000 AI agents working alongside 40,000 humans RIGHT NOW
→ 18 months ago? They had just 3,000 agents
→ By end of 2026, AI agents will equal their human headcount
→ Their entire business model is changing — from hourly fees to outcome-based contracts

The CEO said it bluntly:
"When people ask how many people McKinsey employs, I say 60,000 — 40,000 humans and 20,000 agents."

He counts AI as employees.

Not tools. Not software. Employees.

And they're hiring humans who can work WITH those agents — not humans who compete against them.

This changes everything about what a 'good candidate' looks like.

The student who memorised case frameworks?
Might lose to the one who asked better questions to Lilli.

The analyst who worked 80-hour weeks?
Might be replaced by someone who runs 10 agents in parallel and reviews the output.

This is not a warning. It's a signal.

The firms rewriting the rules aren't waiting for regulations or roadmaps.
They're already operating in the new world.

The question is — are you?

Drop your thoughts below. Especially if you're a student or early-career professional — how are you thinking about this? 👇

🚀 AI adoption in Australia isn’t being led by enterprise.
It’s being driven by small businesses - quietly, and much faster than most people realise.

NAB data shows:
🔹 ~42% of small businesses are already using AI
🔹 Another 14% are planning to adopt
🔹 Most common use case: everyday productivity (not transformation)

Let’s be clear: this isn’t “AI strategy.”
No big programs. No steering committees.
This is operators using AI to get immediate leverage in their day-to-day work.
→ Proposals drafted in minutes
→ Contracts summarised instantly
→ Admin work cut down significantly

👉 One small design firm is using AI to review contracts and flag risks.
Not a legal team - just smart use of tools.
👉 A manufacturing business is using AI as a “low-cost strategist” to analyse documents.

That capability didn’t exist for them 12 months ago.

This is what’s actually happening:
AI is becoming a baseline skill.
Not a specialist function. Not a future capability. A baseline.

And this is where the gap is opening up.
Teams that are experimenting → compounding productivity fast
Teams that aren’t → falling behind quietly

From a hiring perspective, most companies are still thinking about this wrong.

It’s not just about hiring AI specialists.

The real demand shift is here:
→ People who can apply AI inside real workflows
→ Operators who can remove 20–40% of manual effort
→ Domain experts who are AI-enabled by default

The uncomfortable question:
How much of your team’s work today is still manual - and doesn’t need to be?
Because that’s where your competitors are already pulling ahead.
Curious - are you seeing real usage in your team, or still in “exploration mode”?

#ArtificialIntelligence #AIAdoption #FutureOfWork #DigitalTransformation #AIinBusiness #Productivity #AUSBusiness #AITrends #Synchropartners
